coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesized multifunctional carbon nitride dots for fluorescent sensing, bioimaging, and selective cytotoxic effect on cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: R. Thara, Chinnu
Korah, Binila K.
John, Bony K.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metals are one of the significant contributors to environmental pollution; they can occur naturally or through anthropogenic activities. On the other hand, antibiotics are emerging contaminants that can pollute water bodies. In the present work, versatile metal-free oxygen-doped carbon nitrides dots (O-GCNQD) having multiple applications like rapid fluorescent sensing of heavy metal mercury (Hg2+), antibiotic tetracycline (TC), in-vitro cellular imaging, and selective cytotoxicity for cancerous cells have been developed. In the first part of the work, the intrinsic property exhibited by O-GCNQDs is used in the selective and sensitive sensing of Hg2+ and tetracycline. Additionally, iodide ion (I−) sensitivity is employed to reverse fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ inducing ‘on-off’ fluorescence. The metal and drug detection limit was 250 nM and 410 nM, respectively. The sensor could sense more than 98.7 % of respective metal ions and drug molecules in spiked actual water samples. There is a lot of study on carbon dots (CD) in nanomedicine, particularly in imaging and drug administration. Still, there is less focus on its intracellular activity and possible anticancer processes. So in the second part, the biological applications of O-GCNQDs are explored. Their bright green fluorescence in long UV and biocompatibility were investigated for imaging of cancer cells. The cytotoxic studies showed the carbon dots have exceptional selective toxicity towards cancer B16 murine melanoma cells compared to fibroblast cells. The anticancer studies also reveal that our carbon dots could induce cell apoptosis at lower concentrations without external stimuli like light or heat.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride dots
Fluorescent sensor
Cytotoxicity
Bioimaging
Anticancer
Cell apoptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of oxygen doped carbon nitride dots (O-GCNQDs)
Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ion and tetracycline
Morphology and optical properties of O-GCNQDs
Structure and chemical constitution of O-GCNQDs
Bioimaging of cancer B16 murine melanoma cells with O-GCNQD
Selective cytotoxicity of O-GCNQD for cancer cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research Human Resource Development Group
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Chinnu R Thara is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship, and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and Optical properties of O-GCNQDs. (a) Quasi-spherical carbon nitride dots (scale bar 5 nm). (b) Lattice resolved image showing d-spacing (scale bar 1 nm). (c) SAED pattern. (d) Particle size histogram showing average particle size 3.11 nm. (e) UV–vis. absorption and appearance of quantum dot solution in UV and visible light, the inset shows photograph of O-GCNQD appearing brown under visible light and blue, green in UV light. (f) Fluorescence intensity at various wavelengths. (g) Excitation-emission spectrum. (h) Life-time spectra. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Structure and chemical composition of O-GCNQDs. (a) XRD pattern. (b) FT-IR spectrum. (c) Raman spectrum of O-GCNQDs. (d) High resolution XPS spectra C1s. (e) N1s. (f) O1s spectra.

Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and TC. (a) Fluorescent quenching with different metal ions. (b) pH determination for Hg2+ sensing. (c) Quenching corresponding to various Hg2+ concentrations. (d) Fluorescent quenching with different drug molecules. (e) pH determination for TC sensing. (f) Quenching corresponding to various TC concentrations.

Sensing in spiked real water samples. (a) Diagram showing added, found, and recovery percentage of Hg2+ ions in tap and lake water samples. (b) Diagram showing added, found, and recovery percentage of TC in tap and lake water samples.
Confocal microscopic images of B16 murine melanoma skin cancer cells with various O-GCNQD concentrations. (a) 6.25 µg/mL. (b)12 µg/mL. (c) 18 µg/mL.

Selective cytotoxicity of O-GCNQD. (a) Confocal image at higher O-GCNQD concentration (25 µg/mL).(b) Inverted microscopic image of O-GCNQD incorporated B16 murine melanoma cancer cell.(c) Inverted microscopic image of O-GCNQD incorporated fibroblast cell. (d) Cell viability comparison of O-GCNQD incorporated fibroblast cell and B16 murine melanoma cancer cell.

Mechanism of cytotoxicity. Stage I - Incoming of O-GCNQDs. Stage II – O-GCNQDs entering both cells and generating ROS depending on the cell environment. Stage III – Normal cell retain the structure while cancer cell collapse due to uncontrolled production of ROS.
PL intensity, Quantum yield and Life time of the prepared carbon nitride quantum dots.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesized multifunctional carbon nitride dots for fluorescent sensing, bioimaging, and selective cytotoxic effect on cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: First figure shows a white powder which corresponds to the precursors, l-ascorbic acid, and urea. It gets converted to green fluorescent carbon nitride dots (central spherical green sphere) on hydrothermal treatment. The three arrows around carbon nitride dots show its three applications such as fluorescent sensing, bioimaging, and selective cytotoxicity towards cancer cells.

Heavy metals are one of the significant contributors to environmental pollution; they can occur naturally or through anthropogenic activities. On the other hand, antibiotics are emerging contaminants that can pollute water bodies. In the present work, versatile metal-free oxygen-doped carbon nitrides dots (O-GCNQD) having multiple applications like rapid fluorescent sensing of heavy metal mercury (Hg2+), antibiotic tetracycline (TC), in-vitro cellular imaging, and selective cytotoxicity for cancerous cells have been developed. In the first part of the work, the intrinsic property exhibited by O-GCNQDs is used in the selective and sensitive sensing of Hg2+ and tetracycline. Additionally, iodide ion (I−) sensitivity is employed to reverse fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ inducing ‘on-off’ fluorescence. The metal and drug detection limit was 250 nM and 410 nM, respectively. The sensor could sense more than 98.7 % of respective metal ions and drug molecules in spiked actual water samples. There is a lot of study on carbon dots (CD) in nanomedicine, particularly in imaging and drug administration. Still, there is less focus on its intracellular activity and possible anticancer processes. So in the second part, the biological applications of O-GCNQDs are explored. Their bright green fluorescence in long UV and biocompatibility were investigated for imaging of cancer cells. The cytotoxic studies showed the carbon dots have exceptional selective toxicity towards cancer B16 murine melanoma cells compared to fibroblast cells. The anticancer studies also reveal that our carbon dots could induce cell apoptosis at lower concentrations without external stimuli like light or heat.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The enormous release of heavy metal ions and organic compounds from industrial plants to water sources is a worldwide concern due to the acute threat to nature and organisms [1,2]. Mercury (Hg2+) is a significant heavy metal widely used in industry and agriculture [3]. Its toxicity and bioaccumulation can steer to substantial health hazards to the brain, kidney, and neurological systems even at a low concentration [4–6]. Further, tetracycline is a low-cost broad-spectrum antibiotic used to treat clinical infections such as pneumonia, mycoplasma, urogenital tract infection, epidemics, and more. It is an essential component of medical treatment, particularly in animal therapy. Continuous intake of tetracycline and its derivatives may result in antibiotic-resistant bacteria in animals, rendering it ineffective to use as antibiotic. In addition, established antibiotic-resistant bacteria can spread to humans through the food chain, posing a life-threatening threat to humans [7–9]. The traditional analytical techniques are either sensitive or selective but costly, or they are simple to use but lack sensitivity and selectivity. So far, fluorescence spectroscopy has proven to be a powerful optical technique capable of providing a flexible, sensitive, and straightforward detection approach [10–12].

In the last decade, metal-free quantum dots have received much attention in sensing due to their non-toxic and eco-friendly properties compared to conventional semiconductor metal quantum dots. A new type of fluorescent material, graphitic carbon nitride dots (g-CNQDs), has gained tremendous attention owing to strong fluorescence, high stability, water solubility, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity as a metal-free polymeric material, making them good candidates to substitute the standard QDs [13,14].

Furthermore, quantum dots find applications in biological fields like cellular imaging, drug delivery, cancer treatment, etc., due to their intense fluorescence and biocompatibility [15]. Cancer is one such disease whose prompt and precise detection can considerably reduce death rates, so there is a crucial need for proper diagnostic techniques [16–18]. The use of carbon dots (CD) in nanomedicine is now being researched extensively, especially in imaging and drug delivery, but less attention to its intracellular activity and potential anticancer processes. Carbon-based materials, such as carbon fibres, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots, have increased apoptosis and decreased cancer cell proliferation via a range of signalling channels. There are reports on some non-functionalized carbon dots synthesized from green sources exhibiting anticancer activity achieved by inhibiting proliferation, migration, and increased ROS generation in cancer cells [19–21]. Furthermore, carbon dots also show intrinsic photodynamic properties for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Carbon dots can transfer energy to surrounding oxygen under the excitation of a specific wavelength of light to form reactive oxygen species (ROSs), such as 1O2, O2.−, H2O2, and OH, which can kill cancer cells or cause them to apoptosis [22]. Reactive oxygen generation may not be an intrinsic property of a material as such, and sometimes it comes with narrowing to a nano regime. Studies have revealed the direct relation between the surface area and reactive oxygen species generation [23–24]. Numerous reports on metal-free quantum dots have recently been published, boosting their biocompatibility and safety in in-vivo analysis.

We have employed the usual hydrothermal method for synthesizing oxygen-doped carbon nitride dots (O-GCNQDs) from urea and l-ascorbic acid in the present work. One advantage of the synthesis method was the whole reaction, carbon nitride dot formation, and the oxygen doping occurred in a single step with low-cost precursors at ordinary hydrothermal conditions. O-GCNQDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the fluorescent detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+) and tetracycline (TC). Further, in the presence of iodide ion (I−), the quenched fluorescence of O-GCNQD with Hg2+ is recovered, thus exhibiting a fluorescent turn ‘off - on’ process. In addition to the fluorescent sensing studies, cytotoxicity and in-vitro cellular imaging of O-GCNQDs were also investigated. During the cytotoxicity studies and bioimaging process, considerable toxicity of O-GCNQDs toward cancer cells was observed, but it was not seen for normal fibroblast cells. On further research, our material displayed exceptional anticancer properties in addition to a fluorescent probe for cancer cell imaging. To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies with oxygen-doped carbon nitride dots prepared from the precursors like urea and l-ascorbic acid. Additionally, we have successfully explored multiple applications such as fluorescent sensing, off–on sensing, in-vitro imaging, and anticancer property on this single carbon nitride dot. Furthermore, the intrinsic selective cytotoxicity for malignant cells shown by our carbon nitride dot is not noticed elsewhere. Thus, the oxygen-doped carbon nitride dot could function as a fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ ions, tetracycline, and I− ions. Simultaneously it could act as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging and anticancer agent.

Urea, l-ascorbic acid, metal chlorides, namely, mercuric chloride (HgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and chromium chloride (CrCl3), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) of analytical grade, were purchased from Merck (India). Drugs like 5-fluorouracil, doxycycline, tetracycline, amoxicillin trihydrate, cloxacillin and vancomycin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without purification. L929 fibroblast cells and B16 (murine melanoma) tumor cells for skin cancer was initially procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India and maintained in DMEM and MTT assay (M-5655) (Sigma Aldrich, USA).

Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer was used for recording absorption spectra. FT-IR studies were done using the Perkin Elmer-400 FT-IR spectrometer at 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra and lifetime measurements were recorded using the WITec alpha300RA system with 532 nm DPSS laser and Horiba Fluorolog 3 TCSPC with LEDs of wavelength 295, 330, and 570 nm lifetime measurable from < 100 ps to 100 µs lifetimes. The structure determination was done with the powder X-ray diffractometer using Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation of wavelength 1.5406 Å. Morphology and inner texture properties were analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) by a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. The viability of cells for cytotoxicity was evaluated by direct observation of cells by an Inverted Phase Contrast Microscope and followed by the MTT assay method using a microplate reader. Leica SP8 Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope was used for bioimaging of B16 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxygen doped carbon nitride dots (O-GCNQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Urea and l-ascorbic acid were taken in a 2:1 mass ratio and dissolved in 50 mL of double-distilled water. The mixture was stirred for half an hour to achieve a uniform solution. This solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 4 h. The brown solution was filtered through a microporous 0.22 µm membrane and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min [25]. Similar type oxygen doped carbon nitride dots were prepared with different amounts of urea and l-ascorbic acid (Table T1, Supporting Information (SI)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ ion and tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: For a typical assay, 100 µL of O-GCNQDs suspension was diluted with 3.0 mL of double-distilled water, and then added different concentrations of Hg2+ ions and tetracycline separately for each analysis. The mixed solutions were kept static for a few minutes and finally recorded the associated fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 390 nm at room temperature. Tap water from Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, and lake water from Alappuzha, Kerala, were used for real sample analysis. All these collected samples were centrifuged, filtered, and spiked with appropriate concentrations of Hg2+ ions and tetracycline for sensing.

Like the fluorescent detection method, O-GNCQD solution was prepared and 300 µL of 0.001 M Hg2+ ion was added and allowed to equilibrate for a few minutes. Different volumes of 0.001 M iodide (I−) ion solution were added to this fluorescent quenched solution. Recovery of O-GCNQDs fluorescence intensity recorded with excitation wavelength at 390 nm.

The selectivity of O-GCNQDs for Hg2+ ion and tetracycline was analysed in the presence of other metal ions like Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+, and drugs like vancomycin, 5-fluorouracil, cloxacillin, doxycycline, and amoxicillin trihydrate under the indistinguishable conditions. The concentrations of sensed metal ion, drug, and the corresponding interferents were 100 µM. For the interference study, the O-GCNQD suspension was mixed with Hg2+ and tetracycline in the absence and presence of other interfering species with a concentration of three times that of sensed metal ion and drug.

Biological studies like cytotoxicity and anticancer studies were carried out only after the filtration to ensure sterility. The procedure for both the studies was similar, except the cells were different in each case. In both analyses, the seeded and grown cells were treated with varying concentrations of O-GCNQD (100 µg, 50 µg, 25 µg, 12.5 µg, 6.25 µg) and incubated for 24 h. After the incubation period, the cells were checked for viability by direct microscopic observation and MTT assay method. Detailed procedures for the studies are given in the Supporting Information.

12–25 μg of O-GCNQDS was added into 1 mL of DMEM medium containing B16 murine melanoma cells in a 35 × 35 mm plate. The plate was incubated for 10 h at 37 °C in a humidified environment containing 5 % CO2. The fluorescence images and phase-contrast bright-field images were recorded on a Leica SP8 Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope at an excitation wavelength set above 400 nm.

B16 murine melanoma cell line was cultured in a 25 cm2 tissue culture flask with DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, l-glutamine, sodium bicarbonate, and an antibiotic solution containing: penicillin (100 µg ml−1), streptomycin (100 µg ml−1), and amphotericin B (2.5 µg ml−1). After 24 h, the growth medium was removed, freshly prepared sample in 5 % DMEM were five times serially diluted by twofold dilution and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. Non-treated control cells were also maintained.

Urea was chosen as the nitrogen source in carbon nitride dots due to its abundance, activity under thermal treatment, richness in amino, and, lastly, its cheapness. Ascorbic acid was picked as a new oxygen source instead of the usually used citrate salts. l-ascorbic acid had adequate number of oxygen rich groups; also it has antioxidant, and antibacterial properties, which could come into play during anticancer studies. A total of six different ratios for urea to l-ascorbic acid were tried, including the O-GCNQD synthesis (Table 1 , SI). All the quantum dots exhibited significantly less fluorescence compared to O-GCNQD, except carbon dot prepared with ascorbic acid alone (Fig. S1, Supplementary Information). The as prepared carbon nitride dots PL intensity, quantum yield, and life time is provided in Table 1. Stability of O-GCNQD is studied under two conditions like continuous light irradiation upto 60 min and storage stability upto 25 days (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of O-GCNQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Morphology and size of prepared quantum dots were analysed by HRTEM images. O-GCNQDs have a quasi-spherical structure with an average particle size of 3.1 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1 a, d). The lattice resolved images showed interplanar spacing like 0.31 nm and 0.263 ± 0.015 nm, which points to the presence of multiple active planes in the material (Fig. 1b). Further SAED pattern supports this observation of multiple planes from amorphous carbon ([110] and [101]) and graphitic carbon nitride ([002], [100]) (Fig. 1c) [26,27].

The optical properties of the quantum dots were studied with UV–vis and PL spectra. Two absorption peaks at 221.7 nm and 273 nm was obtained in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1e). This probably implies the presence of carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl group and the π-π* electronic transition in the heptazine like compounds having CC and CN bonds of the sp2 carbon domain and the presence of n → π* transition of CO and CN bonds in g-C3N4 [28–30]. Furthermore, carbon nitride dots show highly intense emissions over a range of excitation wavelengths from 310 to 430 nm with maximum intense emission at 450 nm (Fig. 1f), emission wavelength shows red shift at higher excitation wavelength suggesting excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour. Usually, excitation-dependent properties arise from differently sized NPs, different emissive traps, or oxygen-rich groups and defects on the surface [31,32]. And at the same time, not much of a shift in emission wavelength and intensity has been observed at different temperatures and pH (Figs. S3 & S4).

Meanwhile, the excitation and maximum intense emission peaks have emerged at 390 and 450 nm, respectively. These carbon nitride dots display light brown colour under visible light, bright blue and green fluorescence upon excitation in short and long UV (Fig. 1g). The high electron-withdrawing N- and O- rich edges groups originated from the precursors like urea, and l-ascorbic acid is considered the primary provider of the enhanced PL intensity. Using quinine sulphate (54 % in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4, λex = 369 nm) as reference, the fluorescence quantum yield is calculated to be 35.4 %, this is very high compared to bulk g-C3N4 (4.8 %) and comparable to other heteroatoms doped carbon nitride dots [33,34]. The photophysical behaviour of the photoexcited charge carriers on O-GCNQDs was understood from the time-resolved PL measurements (Fig. 1h). The fluorescent lifetimes are calculated by fitting data with exponential functions ((B1*T1 + B2*T2 + B3*T3)/100) and found as 18.58 ns. The triple exponential decays suggest that the source of PL is from three species. The escalated lifetimes of the charge carriers can be attributed to the improved electron transport and/or electronic band. Further, zeta potential measurement reveals that the O-GCNQDs were negatively charged with a value of −31.8 mV at pH 7.0 and the high negative surface charge ensures its stability (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and chemical constitution of O-GCNQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Structural data obtained from XRD shows characteristic carbon dot peak, a broad peak at 22.70 corresponding to the (002) plane attributed to the set of sp2 and sp3 hybridizations of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2 a) [35]. Carbon nitride dot formation is further confirmed by FT-IR (Fig. 2b) vibrational bands from 3400 to 3000 cm−1 correspond to NH/OH stretching. Bands at 1570, and 1410 cm−1are attributed to the usual stretching modes of CN. The band at 2193 cm−1 indicates the presence of a cyano group in the structure. A major band at 1641, 1093.4 and a minor at 991 cm−1 represent CO and CO stretching vibration. 807 cm−1 band is the characteristic peak in carbon nitride compound that occurs due to out-of-plane bending vibration [36,37]. Additional structural information was gained from Raman spectra (Fig. 2c). Typically g-C3N4 shows two bands centred at 1350 (D band) and 1580 cm−1 (G band) caused by the linking with N atoms and graphite-like sp2 carbon. Similarly, OGCNQDs are showing the same D and G bands which are well reported for carbon materials [38].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed for the chemical state and constitution determination of the synthesized oxygen doped carbon nitride dots. The high-resolution C1s spectrum consists of five peaks. Of these peaks at 284.5 eV and 288.2 eV corresponds to the standard reference sp2 bonded carbon of g-C3N4. The rest of them indicate 289.1 eV (COO−), 285.3 eV (N-CN), and 286.3 eV (CO) (Fig. 2d) [39,40]. N1s peak is deconvoluted to two peaks 399.9 eV, and 401.1eV corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (CNC) and graphitic nitrogen ((C)3N). It might appear from graphitic C3N4 structure or the edge amino group (Fig. 2e) [41,42]. O1s peak is divided into two peaks 531.8 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO) (Fig. 2f) [43,44].

Multiple metal ions were analysed to inspect O-GCNQD as a fluorescent probe for metal ion sensing. The fluorescence response of each metal ion was quite different under the same conditions. Some did not affect PL intensity while some slightly reduced it, but no peak shifts were observed. A prominent quenching of fluorescence was observed in the presence of Hg2+ ion. This is due to the “turned off” of fluorescence of the O-GCNQD by the Hg2+ ion (Fig. 3 a). With a hike in Hg2+ ion concentration, the intensity showed a downfall (Fig. 3c), and a linear relationship was obtained by plotting I0/I with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 2–14 µM (Fig. S6) with R2 of 0.9996. LOD was calculated to be 250 nM from the straight-line plot. The selectivity for mercuric ions in the presence of other ions was also studied at concentrations thrice of the sensed metal ion (Fig. S7). Also, the sensitivity at different pH was studied (Fig. 3b). The quenching of the relative intensity varied with pH, and maximum quenching was observed at 7; and further studies were carried out at this pH.

Fluorescence “turns on” with different anions like I−, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, NO3 2− and CH3COO− were tried. Only the iodide ions brought back the fluorescence, not entirely but significantly (Fig. S8). The concentration of I− ions varied to check whether the initial fluorescence could be brought back, but the fluorescence could not recovered to that extent (Fig. S9). With iodide ions, the fluorescence is turned on due to its better affinity for Hg2+ ions. I− ions can competitively bind with Hg2+ ions on the surface of O-GCNQD and extract them to form stable complexes [45]. This way, fluorescence is recovered. O-GCNQD’s Hg2+ sensing is compared with similar reported works (Table 2 (SI)).

Compared to other reports in fluorescent sensing of Hg2+, O-GCNQD exhibit a lower detection limit for Hg2+, which arises from its structure. Fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ is intelligible from its affinity for amino and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon nitride dots. Hg2+ can bind to multiple ‘imine’ (CNC) and hydroxyl groups on the surface, resulting in non-fluorescent complex formation. These complexes aggregate and change the electronic structure of O-GCNQD, resulting in fluorescence quenching [46,47]. Thus a prominent fluorescent quenching action at a lower Hg2+ concentration can bring the detection limit to very less. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of nitrogen shows a shift in the peak corresponding to amino ‘N’ which demonstrates the binding of Hg2+ (Fig. S10). Such complexes thus formed disrupt the radiative electron/hole pair recombination leading to quenching. Also, the lifetime of O-GCNQD decreases when accompanied by mercuric ions (Fig. S11) implicit the mechanism involves is dynamic quenching.

The oxygen-doped carbon nitride dot as a fluorescent probe for drug sensing ended up with potential sensing of tetracycline drug. A total of six drugs samples were analysed for the sensing study, where no other drug except TC exhibited significant quenching of O-GCNQD fluorescence (Fig. 3d). The PL intensity drastically decreased when 100 µM TC solution was added to ∼30 µg/mL of carbon nitride dot solution, suggesting that carbon nitride fluorescence is sensitive to TC. A pH study was essential to determine the stability of O-GCNQDs and the pH-dependent quenching efficiency of TC, which provided the inferences like the relative intensity of O-GCNQDs didn’t show a considerable variation to the acid-base environment supporting its stability. But in the presence of TC; maximum fluorescent quenching was observed at pH 9 (Fig. 3e). Therefore further studies were carried out at pH 9.

A systematic study of the variation of fluorescence intensity of O-GCNQD with an increase in TC concentration was carried out just like Hg2+sensing from 0 µM to 20 µM. With a rise in TC concentration, fluorescence intensity moderately diminished (Fig. 3f). And no peak shifts were observed, which usually happens in some quantum dots due to scattering, photobleaching, etc. When I0/I were plotted against concentration of TC, a linear graph was obtained between 2 µM and 16 µM (Fig. S13) with R2 value 0.9986. The limit of detection (LOD) for TC is 410 nM calculated with the equation LOD = 3σ/S, σ is the slope, and S is the standard deviation. Furthermore, the selectivity of O-GCNQD for TC is determined in the presence of other drugs at a concentration greatly as that of TC (Fig. S12). The quenching due to other drugs was negligible compared to TC except for doxycycline, which belongs to the tetracycline drugs. In the case of O-GCNQD and TC, a shift in absorption peaks from 358.6 nm to 374.8 nm and 273.1 nm to 267.3 nm was observed in UV–vis spectra (Fig. S14). Unlike the reported ones, the lifetime of O-GCNQD decreased in the presence of TC (Fig. S15). These observations point to the dynamic quenching mechanism of the carbon nitride dot [48]. TC sensing is compared with similar reports in Table 3 (SI). The superior fluorescent sensitivity of our material for TC might have evolved from the amino and oxygen-rich groups in the O-GCNQD structure. TC consumes carboxyl or other negative groups on the surface of the carbon nitride dot. Also, through electrostatic interaction, TCs could induce the aggregation of O-GCNQDs. These electronic structural changes induced in O-GCNQDs leads to remarkable fluorescence quenching feature and better sensitivity.

The efficiency of the O-GCNQD for Hg2+, and TC sensing in real waters was tested with some real water samples. The various real water samples (tap and lake water) collected are spiked with known concentrations of Hg2+, and TC. These spiked samples were further analysed by PL spectra. The recovery percentage of Hg2+, and TC in the different water samples were obtained in the range of 98.7–100.58 %, (Fig. 4 a), and (Fig. 4b). These results suggest that O-GCNQDs are a suitable fluorescent probe for the potential sensing of both the analytes in natural water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of cancer B16 murine melanoma cells with O-GCNQD
ce:section.ce:para: The in-vitro imaging of cells ventured only after the cellular toxicity studies carried out on fibroblast cells with various O-GCNQD concentrations to ensure biocompatibility. The viability of cells was evaluated by direct observation of cells by an inverted phase contrast microscope followed by the MTT assay analysis. It was impressive to learn that the cell viability is more than fifty percent even at a concentration as high as 100 µg/mL after 24 h of incubation (Fig. S16).

Since the cytotoxic studies with fibroblasts cells displayed O-GNQDs was non-toxic, imaging of B16 murine melanoma cells were carried out, for which the cancer cells were directly incubated with O-GCNQD for 24 h. B16 skin cancer cells didn’t show any fluorescence in the absence of O-GCNQDs when observed in a confocal microscope at an excitation wavelength above 400 nm. But the same cell, after incubation with carbon nitride dots, showed bright green fluorescence at the same wavelength. From the images, it is pretty clear that O-GCNQDs without any bioconjugation are penetrating the cell by cellular endocytosis. The single-cell analysis (Fig. 5 a) displays a dark region without any illumination in the nucleus where O-GCNQDs could not enter. The brighter green coloured ones are the cellular bodies in the cytoplasm like endosomes, lysosomes, etc., where quantum dots could enter quickly [49,50]. Additionally, fluorescent intensity is lower at a minimum concentration (6.25 µg/mL) and enhances with increase in concentration (18 µg/mL) (Fig. 5a–c). The photostability of quantum dots was investigated by continuous excitation for 20 min which didn’t reduce the fluorescent intensity. On this ground, it can be concluded that O-GCNQDs are biocompatible and suitable for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective cytotoxicity of O-GCNQD for cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: In our case, cellular confocal imaging has paved the way to realize selective cytotoxicity of O-GCNQDs. As mentioned in the earlier section, concentration-dependent fluorescence was checked for B16 murine melanoma cells, and it was clear from the images that cell structure collapsed at higher concentrations (Fig. 6 a). Based on this, the anticancer study of O-GCNQDs on the same cancer cell line was carried out. Fig. 6b and c show the phase contrast image of control and quantum dot incubated (24 h) fibroblast and B16 murine melanoma cells at various concentrations. Almost 50 % of the B16 murine melanoma cells were collapsed by a concentration of 25 µg/ mL, while the fibroblasts maintained the morphology to a great extent. Further MTT assay carried for both cells also points to the selective cytotoxic effect of O-GCNQD (Fig. 6d). LD50 of O-GCNQD for B16 murine melanoma cells and fibroblasts are 49.75 µg/mL and 145.15 µg/mL, respectively (Calculated using ED50 PLUS V1.0 Software).

Since this is the first time such a selective toxic behaviour is observed for unmodified carbon dots, the probable mechanism could be portrayed from similar reports. Cytotoxicity of quantum dots is well known, and many studies have been carried out to know the extent of toxicity, its mechanism, dependence on temperature, pH, light, etc. [51–53]. Being cytotoxic primarily means causing cell damage by generating reactive oxygen species. Reactive oxygen species are highly reactive chemicals formed by oxygen metabolism, essential for cell functioning. At the same time, ROS can be double-headed swords; it can protect and kill a cell in a matter of time, depending on external and internal factors. In the case of cancer cells, modest levels of ROS are responsible for cellular activation, growth inflammation, etc., but higher levels inhibit cell growth. It is the mechanism of action in many chemotherapeutic drugs [54,55]. Clearly, in our case also, cells are collapsed by ROS, but here they are not light or heat-driven like in photodynamic therapy or photothermal therapy.

In some cases, photo-Fenton and Fenton-like reactions produce ROS but such a response is not feasible here as this reaction happens in the presence of a Fe2+ or other transition metal ions [56,57]. Two reports were found where selective cytotoxicity was tried for cancerous and normal cells [58,59]. Khorammi et al. studied the cytotoxicity of green synthesized silver nanoparticles on fibroblasts and MCF-7 tumor cells. Only 14 % cytotoxicity for fibroblast and 70 % for cancer cells was achieved after 48 h for the same concentration. Monteiro et al. studied the dipicolinato-based Eu3+ complex for selectivity. Here also, the standard NIH/3T3 cell line was immune to cytotoxicity compared to NG97 and PANC-1 cancer cells. Both reports suggest the lower penetration of sample into the normal cell and, at the same time, effortless penetrations into the cancer cell are the reasons for selective toxicity. Once entered inside the cell, the particle will interact with cellular constituents, disrupts the electron transfer in them, and create oxidative stress resulting in ROS generation. In addition, there are some reports on pH-dependent ROS generation. Hydroxyl radical is produced more at lower pH and lower at higher pH [60,61]. W.He et al. studied the effect of pH on the generation of ROS in AgNPs [62]. With ESR, they discovered that the hydroxyl radicals were generated between pH 1.2–4.6 and negligible above 7.4. Based on earlier reports mentioned above, we can draw a probable mechanism of selective cytotoxicity involving ROS, where carbon nitride dots has better penetration for cancer cell compared to normal cell due to the difference in the chemical environment between the two. Once inside the cell, they develop oxidative stress generating more ROS and ultimately resulting in cell death. Also, even if O-GCNQD penetrates both cells, due to the lower pH in cancer cells, more and more ROS could be produced, which may not happen in the normal cell due to higher pH. The probable mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 7 .

We have effortlessly synthesized oxygen-doped carbon nitride dots with high fluorescence, quantum yield, and lifetime. In the laboratory, these carbon dots showed magnificent fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity for heavy metal ion mercury (Hg2+) and antibiotic tetracycline (TC) and real samples with LODs of 250 nM and 410 nM, respectively. In the presence of iodide ion (I−), quenched fluorescence of O-GCNQD recovered, mimicking the “off–on” process in fluorescence. Imaging studies displayed bright green fluorescent illuminated cells at excitation wavelength above 400 nm upon intake of O-GCNQD. The concentration-dependent fluorescence imaging of cells indicated the collapsing of cell structure at comparatively higher concentrations. Based upon these imaging results showing the cellular collapse, cytotoxicity studies with O-GCNQD on fibroblast cells and B16 murine melanoma cells were carried out. Results specified that the carbon dots are selectively toxic to cancer cells, and the reason for such selectivity still needs to be understood. But it is believed that the particular cancer cell environment plays a significant role in cell apoptosis by enhanced ROS generation. The anticancer studies with O-GCNQD on B16 murine melanoma cells showed that cell apoptosis could be brought independently without external factors like light and heat. There are only feeble reports on selective cytotoxic behavior and the anticancer effect of carbon nitride dots. Based on the observations, it is pretty clear that our synthesized carbon nitride dot has many potential applications. They range from the simple fluorescent probe for detecting heavy metal ions and antibiotics to an imaging probe for cancer cells, which could act as an anticancer agent for the same cells after a particular concentration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Chinnu R Thara is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship, and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.
The study does not involve the use of humans or animals.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.120809.
